title: Discontinuous Latent Representations
concept: Nothing forces similar inputs to land near each other in latent space, so small movements can produce wildly different, unrealistic outputs.

exercises.prompt: When you slightly move a slider that controls a video game character's face, what would you WANT to happen?

options.text: The face changes just a little bit, smoothly
The face turns into a completely different monster instantly

exercises.explain: Small changes to a setting should usually cause small, smooth changes to the result — that's what we'd expect from a well-organized 'map' of possibilities.

cards.title: One Tiny Nudge... BOOM!
cards.body: Imagine turning a dial just a hair to the left, expecting a cat's ears to get slightly smaller — but instead, the cat turns into a toaster! In some AI models, the 'map' of possible outputs (called latent space) has holes and jumps in it. Nearby points on the map don't always mean nearby-looking results.

cards.title: What Are Discontinuous Latent Representations?
cards.body: Latent space is like a secret map an AI uses to organize everything it can create. Ideally, similar things (like two similar faces) sit close together on this map. But sometimes the map is messy — nothing forces similar inputs to stay neighbors. So a tiny step on the map can suddenly jump to a totally different, weird, or broken output.

cards.title: A Messy Filing Cabinet
cards.body: Imagine a filing cabinet where folders aren't organized by topic. You'd expect 'Dogs' to be next to 'Puppies' — but instead 'Dogs' might be sitting right next to 'Rocket Ships' for no reason. Reaching for the next folder over gives you something totally unrelated. That's what a discontinuous latent space feels like.

cards.title: The Morphing Face Glitch
cards.body: A face-generating AI has a latent space where moving a slider slowly should morph one face into another smoothly. But if the space is discontinuous, sliding just one small notch can make a smiling face suddenly become a distorted blob, because that little gap in the map wasn't trained to be smooth or meaningful.

cards.title: Myth: AI Always 'Understands' Similarity
cards.body: Myth: If two inputs look similar to us, the AI's latent space will always place them close together. Truth: Nothing automatically guarantees this! Unless a model is specifically trained to keep similar things close (like with special techniques), the latent space can have random gaps, jumps, and weird neighborhoods.

cards.title: Smooth Map vs. Broken Map
cards.body: Compare how latent space can behave: a smooth, continuous space changes gradually, while a discontinuous one jumps unpredictably.

left.text: Small step = small, gentle change (cat ears shrink a little)

right.text: Small step = sudden, wild change (cat becomes toaster)

options.text: Nearby points in the map always produce nearby, similar outputs
Nearby points in the map can produce wildly different, unexpected outputs
The AI has no latent space at all
The AI only works with images, never text

exercises.explain: Discontinuous means the map has gaps or jumps — being close on the map doesn't guarantee being close in the actual output.

exercises.prompt: In a discontinuous latent space, a tiny change to the input always produces a tiny, smooth change to the output.
exercises.explain: False! That's the whole problem — tiny changes can sometimes cause huge, unpredictable jumps in the output.

exercises.sentence: Nothing forces similar inputs to land near each other in latent space, so small movements can produce wildly different, ___ outputs.
exercises.explain: Without something enforcing smoothness, small movements can lead to outputs that look broken or unrealistic.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows a discontinuous latent space problem?

options.text: Slightly adjusting a face-generator slider and getting a distorted, glitchy face out of nowhere
Slightly adjusting a slider and getting a face that looks just a tiny bit older
Asking an AI the same question twice and getting the same answer

exercises.explain: The distorted glitchy jump from a tiny slider move is a classic sign of gaps in the latent map.

exercises.prompt: A researcher trains an AI to generate shoe designs. She notices that moving the design slider by just 1% sometimes turns a sneaker into a random blob shape. What is the BEST explanation for this?

options.text: The latent space is discontinuous — nothing guarantees nearby points produce similar shoes
The AI is broken and needs to be restarted
This always happens with every AI and can't be improved
The slider itself is malfunctioning, not the AI

exercises.explain: This is the classic sign of a discontinuous latent space: small input changes aren't guaranteed to produce small, similar output changes, so gaps in the map cause sudden weird jumps.
